info.text: In this demo I’ve imported Paper.js by artist/designers Jürg Lehni & Jonathan Puckey and others, a vector-graphics scripting library for the browser. Paper sits on top of the HTML5 Canvas, but unlike pixel-based drawing libraries such as p5.js, it treats everything you draw as vector geometry, paths, curves, points, and segments. That makes it especially good for projects that require precise, resolution-independent drawing or vector-based workflows.
info.title: getting started
Paper and Illustrator
info.text: Paper.js grew out of Scriptographer, an early scripting plugin for Adobe Illustrator that let designers automate and extend their artwork using JavaScript. Think of it as a programmable version of Photoshop or Illustrator as explained in the Features section of their site. Even though it renders to Canvas, it’s fully fluent in SVG. You can import SVG files directly into Paper.js and you can export your scene back to SVG for use in vector editors or the web.

info.title: global vars
info.text: At the top of our JavaScript I've created three global variables, first path (which will hold our path object), then points to define how many points we want our path to have and segLength to set the length of each segment (the space between each point)

info.title: canvas setup
info.text: We start in a setup function by creating a 600×600 canvas, appending it to the page, and calling paper.setup(canvas). That initiates Paper.js and binds all of its drawing tools and coordinate system to this canvas.

info.title: new Path
info.text: Next we create a paper.Path with a thick red stroke and rounded caps.

info.title: start anchor
info.text: To begin creating our path we first compute a starting anchor, start, by taking the canvas center and dividing it by [10, 1]. That scales the x-coordinate to one-tenth while leaving y unchanged, so the baseline sits well to the left but remains vertically centered.

info.title: segments
info.text: Inside this loop we create a growing horizontal offset i * segLength for each segment. Adding that offset to start gives the absolute position for the current segment, which we then append to the path as a new point with path.add(). Because .add() returns a new point (it doesn’t change start), each iteration cleanly places the next segment exactly segLength pixels to the right of the previous one, all sharing the same y. The result is a straight, evenly spaced horizontal chain that begins near the left side and marches across the canvas.

info.title: onMouseMove
info.text: To make it interactive, we set Paper's onMouseMove to our update function. Whenever the mouse moves over the canvas, Paper will call update passing an event that includes the mouse position in project coordinates.

info.title: update function
info.text: Inside update function we attach the head of the path to the cursor by setting the path's firstSegment to e.point (the current mouse coordinates).

info.title: update segments
info.text: Then we “relax” the rest of the segments: for each pair of neighbors, we compute the vector from the current segment to the next, reset that vector’s length to the fixed segLength, and reposition the next segment accordingly. This algorithmic constraint keeps each segment the same distance apart, like links in a chain.

info.title: smooth
info.text: Lastly, path.smooth({ type: 'continuous' }) recalculates the Bézier handles so the chain curves gracefully rather than showing sharp kinks. The result is a smooth, springy ribbon that follows your mouse with evenly spaced segments.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded.

info.title: lots more
info.text: There’s loads more you can do with this library, this is only the beginning. Paper.js has great reference docs and tutorials, powerful path operations (booleans, offsets), hit-testing, groups, and raster support, plus handy tools for mouse/keyboard interaction. Because it’s vector-based, you can scale artwork cleanly, export SVG, or even mix Paper with Canvas image effects, check out some of their other examples for more insiration.
